reasoning:
The question concerns the WGS10YR (weekly average of daily 10-year Treasury yields, ending on Friday). For the week ending March 27 (the latest known weekly value associated with March 29), the reported value is 4.38%, calculated as the average of the five daily readings (4.34, 4.39, 4.33, 4.42, and 4.44). However, the critical detail is that the most recent daily yield on Friday, March 27 closed at a weekly high of 4.44%. The following week's average (resolving around April 5) will start from this 4.44% baseline. If yields follow a random walk (expectation of future values equals the current value), the expected weekly average for next week is 4.44%. 

For the series to not increase, next week's average must fall below 4.385% (it is rounded to 2 decimals). This requires the daily yield to drop on average by more than 5.5 basis points from Friday's closing level over the course of the week. Historically, the standard deviation of a 5-day weekly average given a starting jump-off point is roughly 1.5 * daily volatility. Assuming typical daily volatility of 7-8 bps, the standard deviation of the new weekly average is about 11-12 bps. The z-score for crossing the 4.385% mark from a 4.44% start is approximately -0.48. This translates to an almost 68-70% statistical probability of the average remaining above 4.38%. 

Moreover, recent geopolitical stress regarding an Iran conflict has sparked inflation concerns, increasing bond yields (as evidenced by news from late March 2026). Yield momentum is solidly upward, further supporting the mathematical edge provided by Friday's high close. Thus, I estimate a 69% probability that the value increases.
